title: #support: 1739563301
preview:
Miguel Santos: Hey folks — got a Dedicated customer asking what the burst denial reason codes mean. They’re seeing intermittent `429` + a `burst_denial_reason` in the response headers (and in the Console panel). What’s the best way for Support to explain these in plain terms + what actions to take? Also, do we have something linkable (runbook / docs) we can send?

SupportBot: :ticket: Linked customer case: SUP-28503 ("429s when burst cap reached")

Sean Gallagher: +1 we should give Support a crisp mapping + “what you should do next”. We also need to be careful to not imply burst is guaranteed capacity.

Peter Holtz: The short version: burst is opportunistic + guarded. Denials happen for a few distinct reasons, and the customer action differs.

Kira Thompson: I can write the customer-facing blurb, but I need the canonical list + recommended retry guidance. @Benji Okafor can you paste the current codes we’ve standardized on?

Benji Okafor: Yep. Current denial reasons (what customer sees) are:
- `BURST_CAP_REACHED`: tenant hit configured burst cap (their max extra concurrency/GPU-seconds above baseline). This is “you used up your allowed burst headroom right now”.
- `POOL_SATURATED`: burst pool for that region/hw class is at/near capacity (can’t safely admit more burst without impacting others).
- `FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN`: global/fleet safety cap tripped (protecting non-burst SLOs). Temporary, usually resolves when load drops.
- `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`: tenant is in cooldown window (anti-thrashing + fairness). Deny until cooldown expires.
- `PRIORITY_DEFERRED`: admitted other higher-priority burst traffic first (tiering + fairness). Typically transient.
- `BURST_DISABLED_BY_POLICY`: burst disabled for tenant/route/model (feature flag, allowlist, or policy gate).
- `CONFIG_INVALID`: tenant config is missing/invalid (rare; generally a control-plane misconfig).
- `TELEMETRY_UNCERTAIN`: we can’t confidently determine safe capacity due to telemetry lag/partial outage, so we fail closed.

Benji Okafor: Not all tenants will see all of these; most common in the wild are `BURST_CAP_REACHED` and `POOL_SATURATED`.

Miguel Santos: Super helpful. How do we want Support to phrase `POOL_SATURATED` without making it sound like our platform is “out of GPUs” broadly?

Peter Holtz: Suggestion:
- “Burst uses a shared Redwood-managed burst pool in your region/hardware class. During peak demand, the pool can be fully utilized. When that happens, we deny additional burst requests to protect performance for all tenants (including your baseline).”

Sean Gallagher: + explicitly: baseline dedicated capacity is still protected; burst is “best effort within guardrails”.

Kira Thompson: For retries: are we recommending immediate retry on all denials? Or only some?

Rafael Mendes: For Support guidance:
- `BURST_CAP_REACHED`: retrying won’t help until usage falls below cap / window resets. Recommend: reduce concurrency, schedule batch job differently, or request higher cap via CSM.
- `POOL_SATURATED`, `PRIORITY_DEFERRED`: retry *can* work. Use exponential backoff + jitter; don’t hot-loop.
- `FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN`: retry after backoff; if persistent > X mins, open Support escalation (SRE oncall) because it indicates neighbor impact risk.
- `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`: retry after a short delay; cooldown is usually on the order of minutes (Support can check the dashboard panel).
- `TELEMETRY_UNCERTAIN`: retry after backoff; if it persists, treat as incident (telemetry pipeline / admission controller health).

Rafael Mendes: We have a runbook draft that covers “how to interpret denial reasons” + “what to check in dashboards”: https://confluence.redwood.internal/eng-sre/runbooks/burst-pool-overload-mitigation-runbook (still WIP, but good enough for internal Support).

Miguel Santos: Nice. Do we have something customer-visible yet?

Kira Thompson: Not a public doc yet; I can add a short section to the Dedicated burst guide once PR-928 is merged. For now, Support can paraphrase + we can share a snippet if needed.

Sean Gallagher: For now please avoid linking internal confluence to customers. Instead: provide a short explanation + offer to review their burst config + suggest backoff behavior.

Benji Okafor: Also: if customer asks “why did I get 429 instead of queueing?” — our semantics are immediate deny (no queue) once guardrails say no. Queueing is intentionally out of scope for v1 to avoid tail-latency blowups.

Miguel Santos: Got it. Any quick checklist Support can follow when customer says “denials higher than expected”?

Peter Holtz: Internal checklist:
1) Confirm their baseline vs burst cap (`burst_max_ratio`, per-tenant cap) in control plane.
2) Check burst pool saturation dashboard for their region/hw.
3) Check `FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN` events + any SLO regression gate alerts.
4) If `TELEMETRY_UNCERTAIN` spikes, check admission controller + telemetry lag panels.

Rafael Mendes: + if they’re hitting `BURST_CAP_REACHED` constantly, it’s often a client-side concurrency spike / retries amplifying load. Recommend: cap parallelism + use idempotency keys to avoid double work.

Kira Thompson: Draft customer-facing phrasing (Support can copy/paste):
“Burst capacity provides temporary throughput above your reserved baseline when shared burst pool capacity is available. If a request is denied with a burst denial reason code, it means one of the guardrails is preventing additional burst to protect performance and fairness. Your reserved baseline capacity remains unchanged. For saturation-related denials, retry with exponential backoff + jitter; for cap-related denials, reduce concurrency or work with us to adjust limits.”

Miguel Santos: Perfect. I’ll respond to the customer with the mapping for the two codes they saw (`POOL_SATURATED`, `BURST_CAP_REACHED`) and include the retry guidance. I’ll also link the internal runbook in the case notes for future.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Please ping if you see `FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN` in a customer case — we want to know ASAP.

Benji Okafor: I’ll add a short “Support glossary” section to the denial reason codes ticket so it stays canonical. :writing_hand:

title: #support: 1739557802
preview:
Jonas Weber: NordBank asking if EU-only residency can *ever* fail over to US. We can do tenant routing allowlist + disable payload logs, but need security signoff. Any objections?
Aisha Rahman: If its time-bounded + documented as an exception, OK. Make sure to state its out-of-region *serving* only (no storage) and that payload logging is disabled.
Priya Natarajan: Legal ok with a short memo to customer (scope/duration/controls). Please dont call it EU-only in writing if were allowing US failover  phrase as EU primary with conditional US failover.
Camila Reyes: Got it. Ill tie to SUP-4187 and send memo once you both approve wording.
Jonas Weber: Implementation-wise Ill add a tenant override: allow us-east only when eu-west degraded or scheduled DR window. Will post config snippet here once merged.

title: #support: 1739478025
preview:
Dev Patel: hey team — as we prep for checklist rollout, can we align on support macros + triage questions keyed off checklist step state?

Dev Patel: goal: faster first response for the common onboarding issues (401/403, streaming disconnects, 429 surprises, “usage dashboard not updating”). if we can map these to step states, we can ask the right Qs immediately.

Priya Shah: +1. right now we ask a bunch of generic stuff and it’s slow. if Console shows “API key created” but “first successful request” not done, we should go straight to auth/env var troubleshooting.

Jasmine Liu: also support needs a clear “what does each step mean” in plain language. some customers will say “i did it” but event never fired.

Dev Patel: sharing a draft macro skeleton. pls critique.

Dev Patel: 
Macro (draft): “Onboarding checklist — triage”
1) Can you share your Redwood org + project name (or project id) and region?
2) Are you using Redwood SDK or OpenAI-compat?
3) What step is the checklist stuck on?
4) Please paste:
   - request URL (redact key)
   - HTTP status + response body
   - request-id / trace-id (if available)
   - your env var names (NOT values)
5) If streaming: what client/runtime (node/python/go) + are you behind a proxy?

Priya Shah: looks good. add a callout for “do not paste API keys”. we still get that.

SupportOps Bot: Reminder: Do not request or collect secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords). Use last-4 / key id where applicable.

Dev Patel: yup, will add.

Priya Shah: can we add checklist-specific branching? like if step shows “First 2xx request” complete but “Streaming established” incomplete, we ask different stuff.

Dev Patel: yep. proposing we publish 1 macro per high-frequency scenario. rough mapping:
- Step: “Create API key” incomplete → guide to Console key creation + permissions
- Step: “Set env + make first request” incomplete → 401/403 + base URL + wrong env var
- Step: “Streaming” incomplete → SSE disconnects, timeouts, proxy buffering
- Step: “Retries/timeouts” incomplete → prod defaults guidance + recommended settings
- Step: “Quotas/budgets” incomplete → 429 after launch + how to view quotas
- Step: “Usage dashboard” incomplete → explain usage lag + where to verify usage

Jasmine Liu: + add “Model pinned / fallback configured” (first prod launch). those are common enterprise questions.

Dev Patel: good call. though I think MVP checklist includes at least model pinning + fallback policy basics.

Kenji Nakamura: from UX side, step names are pretty short in Console. we can mirror the exact labels so support can quote them.

Dev Patel: can you drop the exact step labels as they’ll appear? don’t want drift.

Kenji Nakamura: current copy (subject to final):
- Create an API key
- Make your first request
- Try streaming
- Set timeouts + retries
- Review usage + costs
- Configure quotas / budgets
- Pin a model version
- Set a fallback policy
- Add basic observability (request IDs / logs)

Dev Patel: perfect.

Priya Shah: main pain: sometimes checklist shows wrong state / regresses. if we can’t fix instantly, support needs a way to override a step to unblock customer comms.

Dev Patel: yes — this is the admin override request. we need ability to (a) mark complete (b) reset (c) add note/audit trail. otherwise we end up telling customer “just ignore Console” which is… bad.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve heard this from dogfood. we can add a Support-only override panel, but permissions + audit logging need to be solid.

Chloe Martin: backend side, we can expose an admin endpoint in progress service: `POST /admin/tenants/{tenant_id}/checklist/steps/{step_id}:override` with action + reason. must be restricted to support role + logged.

Logan Wright: +1 but please don’t use override as a fix for event pipeline issues. we should make it clear: override is for customer comms / unblocking, not “hide broken telemetry”. maybe require a reason code.

Priya Shah: agreed. it’s mostly for cases where customer did the thing but event didn’t fire (e.g., first request succeeded via curl but from a different project? or user has multiple orgs).

Dev Patel: exactly. common patterns:
- customer used wrong project key first, then correct one; checklist looks “stuck” even though they’re now live
- usage dashboard lag makes them think nothing is happening; they spam test calls; checklist is ok but costs spike
- streaming step incomplete because client drops connection early; they still got tokens

Jasmine Liu: + account merges / multiple workspaces. checklist progress should be per project, but users think it’s per login.

Dylan Brooks: re override: we can do “mark as complete” and “reset”, but we need to ensure we don’t break the monotonic state model (out-of-order events issue).

Chloe Martin: override can be stored as a separate layer (support_override=true) and UI can show “Completed (Support override)” with a tooltip + link to audit entry.

Kenji Nakamura: that would be ideal. from UX, we should also display “last event seen” for each step (timestamp) in the support view.

Logan Wright: careful with timestamps shown to customers; for support-only view, ok. for customer view, keep it simple.

Dev Patel: can we add a support-only “debug” drawer? not customer visible, but lets us see:
- last onboarding event type + time
- request-id samples
- which SDK/user-agent tag

Chloe Martin: doable, but might be phase 2. MVP: endpoints + audit log + permissions.

Priya Shah: if MVP doesn’t include debug view, can we at least have a macro that asks the customer for the request-id and then we look it up internally?

Dev Patel: yes. updating macro templates:

Dev Patel: 
Macro: “Checklist stuck — Make your first request”
Questions:
1) Which SDK/compat layer?
2) Confirm base URL (Hosted API) + model name used
3) Confirm you set env var `REDWOOD_API_KEY` (or equivalent) and that key is for the same project shown in Console
4) Please send one request-id from a failing call and one from a successful call (if any)
5) Are you seeing 401 vs 403?
Notes for support:
- 401 usually wrong/missing key
- 403 can be project mismatch / key revoked / scope issue
- if step shows complete but customer still errors → likely different project/env

Priya Shah: add “are you using a key with leading/trailing whitespace” — surprisingly frequent.

Dev Patel: lol yes, adding.

Dev Patel:
Macro: “Checklist stuck — Try streaming”
Questions:
1) What language/runtime? Node version? Python requests vs httpx? Go net/http?
2) Are you behind Cloudflare / nginx / corporate proxy?
3) Do you see disconnect after N seconds or immediately?
4) Can you share client-side error + whether you receive partial tokens?
5) Are you using our recommended timeouts/retry preset?
Notes:
- proxies may buffer SSE; need `Cache-Control: no-transform` (if applicable)
- recommend client-side reconnect pattern + backoff

Jasmine Liu: for streaming, we should include a one-liner about “disconnect doesn’t mean request failed; check request-id and usage”. this will reduce panic.

Dev Patel: good.

Priya Shah:
Macro: “429 after launch — quotas/budgets”
Questions:
1) When did you go live? approx QPS + token/sec expected?
2) Which model + context lengths?
3) Are you seeing 429 from rate limit vs quota exceeded? (paste response JSON)
4) Did you configure budgets/quotas in Console?
Notes:
- explain backoff; don’t retry immediately in a tight loop
- if needs higher quota, route to account team / standard process

Dev Patel: yes.

Dylan Brooks: on admin override request — can you file an internal support ticket that describes exact workflow + permission expectations? we’ll link it to ENG work.

Dev Patel: already have INT-3307 open (request support admin override). will add these requirements:
- role-based access (support + CS only)
- audit log includes: who, when, step, action, reason, ticket link
- visible badge in Console: “Support override”
- ability to reset to “auto” (remove override)

Logan Wright: +1 to including ticket link. we can validate override usage volume.

Chloe Martin: for schema, reason codes could be like `CUSTOMER_CONFIRMED`, `TELEMETRY_DELAY`, `EVENT_MISSING`, `MIGRATION/LEGACY`, `OTHER`.

Priya Shah: love it. also please give us a way to “undo” if we mistakenly mark complete.

Chloe Martin: reset supported.

Kenji Nakamura: note: the UI copy “Support override” should probably be “Completed by Support” or similar. “override” sounds scary.

Dev Patel: agree — customer-facing phrasing should be gentle. internally we can still call it override.

Jasmine Liu: what’s the timeline? support enablement needs to know if we’ll have this before cohort rollout.

Dylan Brooks: we can likely ship admin endpoints quickly, UI support view might lag. if needed, support can hit endpoint via an internal tool (or we provide a simple console script) initially.

Dev Patel: ok. for rollout readiness, we’ll:
- finalize macros in Zendesk
- publish a 1-pager: “How to interpret checklist states”
- document escalation path: if checklist wrong → Telemetry (Logan) vs Console (Dylan/Chloe)

Priya Shah: can we also include “expected lag”? like usage dashboard can be up to X minutes behind, so support doesn’t treat it as incident.

Logan Wright: current SLA for usage aggregation is ~5-10 min typical, worst-case 30 min during backfills. we can put that in the macro.

Dev Patel: awesome. I’ll draft the 1-pager + update macros today. Dylan/Chloe pls keep us posted on admin override ETA.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will add the requirements to ENG-5852 and loop you when we have staging.

Jasmine Liu: thanks all. will also coordinate with CS leads so they use the same macro language.

Priya Shah: +1. this will cut our onboarding tickets a lot if we’re consistent.

title: #support: 1739451188
preview:
Dev Patel: got a customer asking for “official rollback guidance” for Private (they’re on-prem-ish but not fully airgapped). they want something they can runbook + get signoff from security. do we have anything shareable yet or should we give the interim steps?

Owen Phillips: +1, this came in as “if upgrade fails, what exact steps restore service + how long does it take” + whether rollback is supported for DB schema changes. they also asked if we can guarantee N-2 -> N with rollback. :grimacing:

Markus Klein: this is Cascade Financial Group (regulated VPC) but they have a strict maintenance window + they mirror artifacts internally. they’re basically asking for what we commit to contractually.

Rafael Mendes: do not promise N-2 -> N right now. current position is N-1 -> N supported path, rollback best-effort + depends on backups. we should point them to the draft runbook + emphasize prechecks/backup required.

Connor O'Brien: yeah, please don’t imply “one command rollback” in current GA. we *do* have rollback command landing in installer (PR 4862) but not in their version yet. we can say “upcoming in next Private release train” and share the high-level semantics.

Kira Thompson: runbook draft is here (internal): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENG/Private+Upgrade+Runbook (WIP)
There’s a section “Rollback (manual)” + “Rollback (installer-managed, upcoming)”. I can paste a sanitized excerpt for support if needed.

Elliot Price: docs PR in flight too (internal/external-ish): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/905
It includes “If you must rollback” guidance but we still have disclaimers around schema migrations.

Dev Patel: what’s the safe interim guidance we can provide today? customer wants steps like: helm rollback? restore db? what about model cache?

Rafael Mendes: interim guidance (today):
1) Stop the upgrade if in progress (don’t keep retrying migrations).
2) Roll back control-plane helm releases to previous chart version.
3) Restore DB from snapshot taken immediately pre-upgrade (or logical dump).
4) Verify CP health checks + auth.
5) Data plane: restart pods, clear incompatible caches if needed.
Key: this only works if you have a clean pre-upgrade backup; otherwise it’s “engagement required”.

Connor O'Brien: and emphasize they must take backup *before* running `upgrade apply`. We’ve seen tickets where ppl assume helm rollback reverts DB. It doesn’t.

Owen Phillips: can we give them exact commands? or do we keep it at “consult runbook / contact support”?

Kira Thompson: I’d avoid putting exact `kubectl` / `helm` commands in an email until we confirm their deployment layout (namespace, release names, storage backend). but we can give a template:
- `helm history <release>`
- `helm rollback <release> <revision>`
- restore procedure depends on postgres deployment (operator vs external)
Also call out that rollback may require downtime.

Elliot Price: agree. We can share a “high-level rollback sequence” as customer-facing text, then say support will provide environment-specific commands during the window.

Markus Klein: they also asked about audit trail / evidence for “rollback invoked” for compliance.

Connor O'Brien: audit log events are being added (PR 29187) but again: not in their current build. For now: we can provide installer logs + timestamps + the backup ID. If they need evidence for auditors, we can recommend collecting the support bundle.

Supportbot: Reminder: please don’t share internal Confluence links with customers. Use approved snippets or the customer docs portal.

Dev Patel: ok. can someone give me a customer-safe paragraph I can drop into the ticket + offer a call?

Kira Thompson: draft text (customer-safe):
“Redwood Private supports safe upgrades with preflight validation and a required pre-upgrade backup. If an upgrade fails and service must be restored quickly, rollback is performed by reverting the control-plane deployment to the prior version and restoring the pre-upgrade database snapshot/backup. Because database schema changes cannot always be reversed automatically, a valid pre-upgrade backup is required for deterministic rollback. We recommend scheduling a maintenance window and testing rollback in a non-production environment. Redwood Support can provide environment-specific commands and verification steps during the upgrade window.”

Rafael Mendes: + add: supported upgrade path is N-1 -> N; larger jumps are evaluated case-by-case.

Elliot Price: I’ll add a “Rollback guarantees / supported paths” callout to the public-facing doc draft so we’re consistent.

Dev Patel: perfect. Owen + I will respond with that, and we’ll offer an assisted upgrade/rollback drill.

Owen Phillips: thanks. I’ll also ask what storage backend they use (EBS vs CSI) since that changes snapshot guidance.

Connor O'Brien: loop me in if they push for contractual guarantees; we should route to Vivek/PM. :thumbsup:

Markus Klein: I’ll ping Vivek to make sure we align with what Sales is saying for Cascade. They’re sensitive on “rollback supported” language.

Dev Patel: done. closing the loop here once customer replies.

title: #support: 1739385502
preview:
Jules Ortega: Heads up escalation from SUP side. Customer is seeing confusing `500` errors after enabling EU-only region pinning. They expected a clear “residency blocked” error but we’re returning generic 500 (esp on streaming).

Jules Ortega: Details:
- Customer org: **Nordholm Payments** (regulated payments)
- Policy: project pinned to `eu-west-1` only (no cross-region fallback)
- Symptom: `POST /v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true` returns `500` with body `{"error":"internal_server_error"}` (no error_code)
- Non-streaming sometimes returns our new 409-ish payload, but streaming path is inconsistent.

Jules Ortega: Repro (from customer + I confirmed in staging with their config):
1) Enable pinning EU-only
2) Use API key scoped to project
3) Call:
```bash
curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer rw_live_***" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model":"redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "stream": true,
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"hello"}]
  }'
```
4) We get `HTTP/1.1 500` and stream closes.

Jules Ortega: Request IDs from prod (customer provided):
- `req_01HNW7V9G5Y7S1B8Q8Q6K8QFRA`
- `req_01HNW7X0X2K9F1T4T9A8K1N3P2`

Jules Ortega: They’re blocked on security review because they need deterministic residency enforcement + clear error code/status for their client handling. Can someone from gateway take a look? @Amara Diallo @Brian Murphy

SupportBot: :rotating_light: Linked ticket: SUP-28577 ("streaming endpoint returns generic 500 instead of residency error")

Amara Diallo: yep this matches what we suspected last week. streaming path is going through the SSE writer and we’re not mapping policy exceptions to the public error contract there. Non-streaming hits the normal error middleware.

Amara Diallo: Jules can you confirm whether the 500 happens only when routing *would have* fallen back cross-region? i.e., primary EU region at capacity?

Jules Ortega: From the customer’s logs + our internal note: yes, looks like EU pool was saturated and router attempted US fallback, which should be blocked by policy. Instead of returning the residency violation payload, the stream handler throws and we end up with generic 500.

Sean Gallagher: I pulled metrics around those req ids. Both correspond to a `routing.cross_region_attempt=1` event and then `gateway.streaming_write_error=1`. So agree it’s not an actual internal crash, it’s our error surfacing.

Brian Murphy: :+1: This is on us. The exception type from policy engine is getting wrapped (looks like `PolicyDeniedError` -> generic `UpstreamError`) before it reaches the SSE error serializer.

Brian Murphy: Expected contract per ADR is:
- HTTP status: `409`
- error_code: `RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION`
- include `request_id` and a help link
- message should say which region was selected vs allowed

Brian Murphy: Current behavior on streaming is “start stream, then hard close with 500” which is worst of both worlds.

Amara Diallo: I can take a patch today. Plan:
1) Ensure residency denial bubbles up as `ResidencyViolation` (no wrapping)
2) In streaming mode, before sending any tokens, do routing+residency evaluation and if denied, return normal JSON error response with 409 (no SSE start)
3) If denial happens after stream started (shouldn’t, but belt+suspenders), send an SSE `event: error` with the standardized payload + then close.

Jules Ortega: That would solve it. Customer wants a *machine-readable* code; their gateway retries on 5xx and it’s causing noisy retries + confusion.

Naomi Feldman: Please make sure the copy matches what we aligned on in #product. We should keep it actionable:
- “Your project is pinned to EU-only; request attempted to route to US due to capacity/fallback; update allowed regions or retry later.”
But avoid mentioning internal capacity details too explicitly.

Brian Murphy: Agree. We can use the templated message we already have for non-streaming:
"Request blocked by data residency policy. Allowed regions: [EU]. Selected region: [US]."
+ remediation:
"Update your residency settings or remove region pinning for this key/project."

Sean Gallagher: also please include the `policy_id` only if we decided to expose it publicly. i remember there was debate.

Brian Murphy: For now we’re not exposing raw policy_id in public response (internal logs only). We can include `residency_policy_scope=project` if needed, but keep identifiers internal.

Amara Diallo: Works. I’ll add `error_code`, `request_id`, `help_url` and keep policy id only in structured logs.

Jules Ortega: What’s ETA for prod? They’re asking if we can give them a date for “fix the 500s”.

Amara Diallo: If patch is small, can land today and ship behind `residency_error_contract_streaming_v1` flag. Could roll to 5% tomorrow and then ramp.

Sean Gallagher: I’m good with flag rollout. Please also add an alert on `gateway.streaming_write_error` correlated with `RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION` so we catch regressions.

Brian Murphy: I’ll update SUP with interim workaround suggestion:
- Temporarily use non-streaming (`stream=false`) if they need deterministic handling until fix rolls.
But we should be careful: they may need streaming for UX.

Jules Ortega: yep, they do want streaming in prod. But they can accept short-term non-streaming for POC if we give them timeline.

Naomi Feldman: Please loop me in on what we tell them—this is the exact behavior we promised in the security review deck.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: I’ll post in this thread when the PR is up and when it’s in staging. Jules, if you can re-test with the same policy config once I ping, that’d be great.

Jules Ortega: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #support: 1739302219
preview:
Dev Patel: Got a customer ask I need help with. Helio Bank security reviewer is asking for “proof of 100% coverage for sensitive actions” for audit logs. They specifically want a screenshot/export from our coverage dashboard + GRC-approved wording we can paste into the response. Any chance someone can grab a current screenshot and blessing on phrasing?

Liam O'Rourke: +1 this is in the procurement queue and they’re blocking on it. They asked if we can attest that all RBAC + credential lifecycle + policy changes emit events across Hosted + Private.

Dev Patel: Context: they’re fine with “at-least-once + de-dupe by event_id” as long as we can show we track coverage and have an exceptions process. They asked for “an example report” they can attach internally.

title: #support: 1738781122
preview:
Dev Patel: hey folks — pulling together the “top onboarding tickets” list for the new Console checklist. Can people drop the most common failure modes you’re seeing + what we wish the product/docs did proactively? Trying to map each one to a checklist step + deep link. :memo:

Priya Shah: yep. Top 3 buckets for Hosted API new users:
1) 401/403 (bad key, wrong env var name, pasted secret key w/ whitespace, using org key in personal acct, etc.)
2) 429 surprises (they run a quick load test or accidentally loop, then think “service is down”)
3) streaming disconnect confusion (works once then disconnects in local dev; they don’t handle reconnect / they think they got partial response and it’s our fault)

Priya Shah: More detail from the ticket patterns:
- 401: they set `REDWOOD_API_KEY` but SDK expects `REDWOOD_API_KEY` (ok) — the REAL issue is they exported in one shell and run in another / container. Or they used the *Console publishable key* vs secret key. Also sometimes they include quotes in env var value.
- 403: key belongs to different workspace than the project they’re hitting; or they created key but didn’t switch to correct org in Console.
- For both: error message is too opaque + docs bury “common mistakes.”

Dev Patel: this is perfect. Checklist step could be “Create API key + set env var” with an acceptance check (Console can verify they created a key) and a docs link to “401/403 troubleshooting”. Also maybe we show the exact env var snippet per OS.

Jasmine Liu: +1 on 401/403. We also see “my first request works in curl but fails in SDK” because they have multiple keys in `.env` and the SDK loads the wrong one. Or they’re calling the OpenAI-compat base URL but using Redwood key (or vice versa). If the checklist can force a “test request” from Console or copy/paste a known-good curl w/ the correct base URL, that would cut a lot of back/forth.

Dev Patel: yep, we should include base URL as part of “First request” step. (Also something like “Confirm you’re hitting `api.redwoodinference.com` not `api.openai.com`”).

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: streaming disconnect tickets often correlate with clients not setting read timeouts correctly or not handling intermediary proxy timeouts (esp. behind corp gateways). People see `ECONNRESET` and assume infra issue.
Checklist should include “Set production timeouts + retries” and “Streaming: handle reconnect + idempotency.” Even a tiny note like “expect occasional disconnects; resume/retry safely” would reduce panic.

Priya Shah: another recurring one: “usage dashboard doesn’t show my calls” (usually lag). They then open billing tickets thinking we’re not metering correctly.
If checklist has a “View usage” step, we should warn “usage can lag up to X minutes” and link to status / data freshness note.

Dev Patel: yes. This one eats a lot of cycles because user is anxious + we end up pulling logs.

Wei Chen: docs side: we can add a “Common onboarding errors” section to the API key + first call docs page. I’ve got draft copy for:
- 401 invalid key (wrong key type, whitespace, env var not loaded)
- 403 forbidden (wrong org/project)
- 404 model not found (typo, region mismatch, deprecated model id)
If you share the exact error strings users paste, I’ll mirror them and add “what to do next”.

Priya Shah: here are actual snippets from recent tickets (sanitized):
- `401 Unauthorized: invalid_api_key`
- `403 Forbidden: workspace_mismatch`
- `429 Too Many Requests: rate_limit_exceeded`
- streaming: `socket hang up`, `ECONNRESET`, sometimes “server closed the connection”
- `request_timeout` when they do long generations w/ default 30s timeout

Dev Patel: nice. For 429 specifically, what do we want checklist to push?

Priya Shah: recommended behavior we keep repeating:
- implement exponential backoff w/ jitter
- honor `Retry-After` if present
- don’t retry non-idempotent requests blindly (or supply idempotency key)
- check quotas/limits in Console before doing load tests
Also: they confuse “rate limit” with “billing limit” — they’ll say “I added a card why do I get 429?”

Monica Patel: SDK angle: we can bake in “production-safe defaults” so users don’t have to learn this the hard way.
- sensible timeouts per endpoint (chat vs embeddings)
- default retries for 429/5xx w/ jitter, but disabled for streaming unless they opt in
- documented reconnect helper for streaming (TS/Go)
If the checklist step links to language-specific snippets, it’ll be consistent.

Dev Patel: +1. Also we need the checklist to be explicit that *streaming requires different retry behavior*.

Dylan Brooks: on Console checklist: we can add deep links for:
- API Keys page (create key)
- “Usage” dashboard
- “Quotas & budgets” page (if enabled)
- “Observability / Logs” view
For 403 workspace mismatch, we can also show current workspace context in the checklist drawer + a “switch workspace” shortcut. (If that’s ok w/ design.)

Logan Wright: telemetry note: if we want the checklist to proactively catch 429 confusion, we can add an event when user first hits 429 and surface a “You’re hitting limits — review quotas/backoff guidance” hint. Same for 401/403 (first auth error). That can also feed the support macros (“we see auth_error events”).

Dev Patel: that would be huge. Support today only sees the ticket text.

Jasmine Liu: one more: “streaming works in Console playground but not in my app” — usually because the playground uses websocket/SSE correctly and their code reads the response body incorrectly (buffering the whole thing). Checklist step could include a minimal streaming example + a warning about proxies/buffers.

Priya Shah: + “model selection confusion” — new user picks a model from docs, then tries to call a different id in code. Or they don’t pin a version and later see different outputs and think it’s unstable.
Checklist step: “Select model + pin version” + mention fallback policy (if you care about uptime).

Dev Patel: good call. That also maps to “first production launch” (stability + reliability).

Sean Gallagher: re: fallback: we should be careful not to encourage everyone to set aggressive fallbacks that mask real issues. But at minimum, step should say “Decide on fallback policy” and link to best practices.

Wei Chen: I can add a docs page section “Model pinning & rollouts” with:
- pin by model version
- recommended rollout flow (dev -> staging -> prod)
- what fallback does / doesn’t do

Dev Patel: summarizing for the checklist mapping (tell me if I missed anything):
- Step: Create API key + set env vars → covers 401/403 basics + correct base URL
- Step: Make first successful request (non-streaming) → validates auth + model id
- Step: Streaming setup → covers SSE handling + disconnect expectations
- Step: Retries/timeouts/idempotency → covers request_timeout, 429, transient 5xx
- Step: Quotas/budgets → preempts 429/billing confusion
- Step: Usage dashboard → set expectation about lag + how to verify requests counted
- Step: Pin model version + decide fallback policy → stability + “why did outputs change?”
- Step: Observability hooks (request IDs/logs) → makes support triage faster

Priya Shah: looks right. One request: can we add a “copy request ID” affordance in Console logs and in docs we tell them to paste `x-redwood-request-id` into tickets? Would reduce back-and-forth a lot.

Dylan Brooks: we can add that to the checklist “Observability” step: “Confirm you can capture request IDs” + deep link to Logs page.

Logan Wright: if gateway emits an event w/ request_id + tenant_id, support could also look it up (permissions permitting). But for MVP, docs + UI affordance is simplest.

Dev Patel: awesome. I’ll turn this into the “Top onboarding failures” section + support macros. Will loop back with draft copy for review. :thumbsup:

title: #support: 1738621175
preview:
Liam O'Rourke: got a customer q from Acme (Splunk) that I want to answer crisply. they’re asking: “if Splunk HEC returns 429/5xx and Redwood retries, should we expect duplicates? do you provide dedup?”

Miguel Santos: +1 they’re worried about incident response workflows. if duplicates happen they don’t want to miss real events or inflate alerting.

Colin O'Donnell: for context, we’ve seen HEC occasionally accept the TCP connection then time out on response during load tests. customer is asking if that results in re-send.

Jordan Lee: yep. current exporter semantics are at-least-once. so duplicates are possible during retries/timeouts. we try hard to minimize but we can’t claim exactly-once.

Jordan Lee: we include stable `event_id` on every audit event (schema v2). that’s the primary dedup key downstream.

Kevin Osei: ^ we should be explicit: delivery guarantee = at-least-once; dedup responsibility is on the SIEM side using `event_id`. also mention we don’t send PII beyond what’s in the schema, but that’s separate.

Liam O'Rourke: can you share the “customer safe” wording? specifically: when do we retry + what do we recommend they do in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: retries:
- non-2xx from HEC (incl 429) => retry with exponential backoff
- network error / timeout => retry (because we don’t know if HEC ingested)
- we batch events; a failed batch is retried as a unit (so duplicates may be at batch granularity)

Jordan Lee: recommended Splunk approach:
- treat `event_id` as unique identifier
- dedup in searches/alert rules using `event_id`
Example SPL:
```spl
index=<redwood_index> sourcetype="redwood:audit"
| dedup event_id
```
If they want persistent dedup across time windows, they can use a lookup/kvstore keyed on `event_id`, but most folks just dedup in detection searches.

Colin O'Donnell: also worth calling out: we set a consistent sourcetype + source (customer-configurable in our UI). doesn’t change the dedup story but helps them scope searches.

Sean Gallagher: add an ops note: if HEC is hard-down or rate-limiting heavily, export lag will increase. we alert internally and customer can see “lag / last success / last error” in Console. we don’t drop silently; worst case it backs up + retries.

Miguel Santos: does exporter support Splunk HEC “indexer acknowledgment” (`ack`) to reduce duplicates?

Jordan Lee: not in beta. we’re doing standard HEC event POST. ack can help confirm indexing but it’s extra complexity + has perf considerations; roadmap item, not something we should imply.

Kevin Osei: +1. keep message: at-least-once, stable `event_id`, customer dedup in SIEM. do not promise “no duplicates”.

Liam O'Rourke: cool. proposed response to customer:

“Redwood audit exports are delivered with *at-least-once* semantics. If Splunk HEC returns an error (e.g., 429/5xx) or we encounter a timeout, we will retry with backoff to avoid losing events. In those retry scenarios, it’s possible for Splunk to receive duplicate copies of some events. Every audit event includes a stable `event_id`, which you can use to deduplicate in Splunk searches/alerts (e.g., `| dedup event_id`). We also surface export health (lag / last success / last error) in the Redwood Console so you can monitor downstream throttling/outages.”

Liam O'Rourke: anyone see issues w that language?

Sean Gallagher: looks good. maybe add: “we do not guarantee exactly-once delivery” explicitly, so it’s unambiguous.

Colin O'Donnell: +1. and if they ask about “missing events”, remind them lag/backfill window exists + we can replay from DLQ if needed (support assisted for beta).

Liam O'Rourke: awesome, sending. :thumbsup:

- we batch events; a failed batch is retried as a unit (so duplicates may be at batch granularity)

Jordan Lee: recommended Splunk approach:
- treat `event_id` as unique identifier
- dedup in searches/alert rules using `event_id`
Example SPL:
```spl
index=<redwood_index> sourcetype="redwood:audit"
| dedup event_id
```
If they want persistent dedup across time windows, they can use a lookup/kvstore keyed on `event_id`, but most folks just dedup in detection searches.

Colin O'Donnell: also worth calling out: we set a consistent sourcetype + source (customer-configurable in our UI). doesn’t change the dedup story but helps them scope searches.

Sean Gallagher: add an ops note: if HEC is hard-down or rate-limiting heavily, export lag will increase. we alert internally and customer can see “lag / last success / last error” in Console. we don’t drop silently; worst case it backs up + retries.

Miguel Santos: does exporter support Splunk HEC “indexer acknowledgment” (`ack`) to reduce duplicates?

Jordan Lee: not in beta. we’re doing standard HEC event POST. ack can help confirm indexing but it’s extra complexity + has perf considerations; roadmap item, not something we should imply.

Kevin Osei: +1. keep message: at-least-once, stable `event_id`, customer dedup in SIEM. do not promise “no duplicates”.

Liam O'Rourke: cool. proposed response to customer:

“Redwood audit exports are delivered with *at-least-once* semantics. If Splunk HEC returns an error (e.g., 429/5xx) or we encounter a timeout, we will retry with backoff to avoid losing events. In those retry scenarios, it’s possible for Splunk to receive duplicate copies of some events. Every audit event includes a stable `event_id`, which you can use to deduplicate in Splunk searches/alerts (e.g., `| dedup event_id`). We also surface export health (lag / last success / last error) in the Redwood Console so you can monitor downstream throttling/outages.”

Liam O'Rourke: anyone see issues w that language?

Sean Gallagher: looks good. maybe add: “we do not guarantee exactly-once delivery” explicitly, so it’s unambiguous.

Colin O'Donnell: +1. and if they ask about “missing events”, remind them lag/backfill window exists + we can replay from DLQ if needed (support assisted for beta).

Liam O'Rourke: awesome, sending. :thumbsup:

title: #support: 1738182203
preview:
Jenny Alvarez: We’ve got a couple Private prospects asking for an “official” backup/restore runbook we can share. Do we have something customer-ready yet, or are we still pointing to internal Confluence?

Samir Patel: +1. Specifically they want:
- what’s included in backup
- how to run it (commands)
- where artifacts live (S3/NFS)
- what Support can help with if restore fails

Markus Klein: For FinBank I’ve been sharing a draft PDF-ish export from Confluence + the installer CLI snippets, but it’s definitely not “Support-ready” (too many internal links + assumes Redwood SRE).

Jenny Alvarez: Right now Support is getting asked “can you validate our IAM?” and “can you tell us how to configure KMS policies?” and we need a consistent boundary.

Kira Thompson: We have internal runbooks in Confluence that are pretty close. Customer-facing version is in progress via docs PR.
Current internal pages:
- Day-2 overview: /eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/private-backup-restore-overview
- Control plane backup runbook: /eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/runbook-control-plane-backup
- Control plane restore runbook: /eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/runbook-control-plane-restore
But they include a lot of “contact SRE” steps.

Elliot Price: Customer guide is being finalized in the docs repo (private backup/restore + DR checklists). Target is a single doc with:
- prerequisites
- supported storage backends
- step-by-step backup and restore
- troubleshooting section
We can share a pre-release link with Support, but I’d rather we don’t send Confluence pages directly to customers.

Samir Patel: Can we at least give Support a snippet we can paste that’s consistent? Like “here’s what we can/can’t advise” + where to point.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE perspective: Support should *not* be telling customers to do cluster-level recovery (etcd restores) or anything that changes Kubernetes state outside the documented flow.
If they’re asking “restore my cluster” → that’s Professional Services / SRE engagement.
If they’re asking “how to run `backup create` / `restore apply` and read the validation report” → Support can help.

Marcus Lin: +1 on boundaries. Also please avoid advising on:
- customer-specific KMS key policy design
- how to configure HSM integrations
- changing encryption settings without security review
We can provide *requirements* (encryption at rest, customer-managed keys supported) and *reference patterns* (Terraform module examples) but not bespoke policy consulting in Support.

Jenny Alvarez: Makes sense. Can we agree on a standard Support statement?

Kira Thompson: Draft for Support macro:
“Support can assist with using Redwood-provided backup/restore tooling (commands, expected outputs, known errors) and interpreting Redwood validation reports. Support cannot provide prescriptive guidance for customer infrastructure design (IAM/KMS policies, network/firewall rules, Kubernetes cluster recovery) beyond Redwood reference documentation. For architecture-specific guidance or hands-on restore assistance, we recommend engaging Redwood Solutions Engineering / Professional Services.”

Markus Klein: Works for me. For customer workflows we should point to:
- storage backend setup guide (S3+KMS / NFS)
- the ‘what’s backed up / what’s out of scope’ section
Also: customers keep assuming “data plane model cache” is backed up. We should explicitly say data plane is treated as stateless; we back up control plane + config state.

Elliot Price: That’s in the doc. Key bullets we can expose:
Backed up:
- control plane config DB + platform config state
- Helm release values/manifests (for deterministic redeploy)
- references to secrets (not raw secret material)
Not backed up:
- customer application data
- cluster-level state unless customer uses separate K8s backup tooling
- GPU node images / ephemeral caches

Samir Patel: Any chance we can share the exact CLI commands today? We keep getting “what do I run?”

Kira Thompson: Current installer commands (customer-safe):
`redwood-private backup create --output <backend-uri> --encryption-provider <kms|local>`
`redwood-private restore apply --input <backend-uri> --validate`
Caveat: flags may change slightly; docs will be source of truth.

Sean Gallagher: Please add: “Always run restore in a clean/staging cluster first” + “don’t run restore on prod without a change window.” Also: validation step is required before we say restore succeeded.

Jenny Alvarez: :thumbsup: Elliot—can we get Support access to the pre-release customer doc link and a short “do/don’t” list?

Elliot Price: Yes. I’ll drop a link in #support-internal once PR is in reviewable state. For now, use Kira’s macro + point to Markus/SE for anything infra-specific.

Marcus Lin: One more note: if customer asks “can we put backups in unencrypted NFS?” answer should be no; encrypted at rest is a requirement. For NFS we require filesystem-level encryption or an encrypted artifact before it hits the share.

Markus Klein: Also, please don’t promise specific RPO/RTO without checking their deployment archetype. We have a matrix by archetype and component.

Samir Patel: Got it. Can we add an internal Support checklist?
- confirm deployment type (VPC single-region / multi-AZ / on-prem / air-gapped)
- confirm backup backend
- confirm encryption provider
- collect last backup manifest + validation report

Jenny Alvarez: Perfect. I’ll turn this into a Support SOP + macro and loop back if we get more customer asks.

Elliot Price: Action item on me: provide customer-ready link + ensure troubleshooting section calls out common failures (RBAC denied, missing CRDs, slow object storage, clock skew) so Support has something to cite.

Sean Gallagher: Action item on SRE: confirm we’re aligned on what failures should page SRE vs stay in Support (restore drills / staging vs customer prod). We’ll keep Support posted.

Jenny Alvarez: Thanks all. :pray: (and sorry for the fire drill)

title: #support: 1738099904
preview:
Jamie Alvarez: Heads up from Acme AI (route experiments beta) — they’re confused by the variant headers we return.

They pasted:
- `x-redwood-experiment-id: exp_9f2...`
- `x-redwood-experiment-variant: b`

Questions from them:
1) is `variant` the model version? (they assumed “b” == “baseline”)
2) can they force a variant by passing the header back in the request?
3) they expected it in the *streaming* responses too (they only see it on the initial HTTP response)

They’re asking what to log / how to debug assignment.

SupportBot: (reaction) :eyes:

Marissa Cole: thanks Jamie. this is on us — we never really documented what these headers mean vs “rollout” model version headers.

Dylan Brooks: +1. We should be explicit that the header is *assigned variant id* (not model version), and it’s not a request override.

Also streaming: headers are only on the initial response (before the chunks). That’s normal HTTP behavior but we should call it out.

Felix Schneider: can we add a tooltip in Console on the “Variant” column (Results view + Experiment details) that says:
- “Variant is the experiment bucket, not the model version.”
- “To see which model served a request, use Model breakdown / request traces.”

Kenji Nakamura: yep. send me the exact customer confusion quote? I can draft microcopy for tooltip + an empty-state callout in the “How to read results” panel.

Jamie Alvarez: customer wording (from the email):
> “We see `x-redwood-experiment-variant: b` and assumed it’s the ‘baseline’ model. Is this telling us which model the request hit? Can we pin the client to variant A for QA by sending the header ourselves?”

Chloe Martin: re (2): we should be careful here: we *do* support overriding assignment internally for debugging (the debug endpoint), but we intentionally did not expose a public “force variant” header.

We should answer: “No, assignment is deterministic based on stickiness key; you can *influence* by choosing a stable stickiness key; you can’t override with variant header.”

Hannah Rivera: also worth mentioning: if they want a “QA pin”, recommended approach is: create a dedicated test user / test org id and use that as stickiness key. Then they’ll deterministically land in one bucket (assuming split + hashing). But that’s not guaranteed unless they control the key.

Logan Wright: we should add a short snippet to docs:
- where to find headers
- what to log
- and a warning: do not treat header as user input; it’s informational

Also: if they’re logging the header per request, it will help us triage attribution drop cases.

Dylan Brooks: agree. On naming: header name is already shipped (`x-redwood-experiment-variant`). Renaming now is messy.

But we can clarify in docs: “`variant` is an opaque identifier (e.g. `a`, `b`, or `control`, `treatment-1` depending on how you configured it).”

Marissa Cole: action items:
1) Docs: add a “Debugging: experiment headers” section with Q&A (what it is / what it isn’t). @Jamie Alvarez can you link the support macro to that once it’s merged?
2) Console: tooltip + maybe a small “What is a variant?” info link near the header values.

Felix Schneider: I’ll take Console tooltip + copy. Will file a small ticket under Console experiments follow-ups.

Kenji Nakamura: I’ll draft copy today and drop in Figma comments + send to Felix.

Jamie Alvarez: yep I’ll update the macro once we have a stable docs URL. For now I’m replying manually with: headers are informational + initial response only for streaming.

Chloe Martin: one more nuance: for Python SDK, they might not be seeing headers because they’re using streaming generator and not inspecting `response.headers` on the initial request.

We should add a snippet for TS + Python:
- TS: `res.headers.get('x-redwood-experiment-variant')`
- Python: `resp.headers.get('x-redwood-experiment-variant')` (before iterating the stream)

Dylan Brooks: good call. @Logan Wright do we have a canonical header list somewhere? I remember we also add `x-redwood-experiment-id` and (optionally) `x-redwood-route-id`.

Logan Wright: canonical list is in the attribution ADR, but not customer-facing. For customer docs we can keep it small:
- `x-redwood-experiment-id`
- `x-redwood-experiment-variant`

and note: headers may be omitted if no experiment applies.

Marissa Cole: ok. @Jamie Alvarez can you reply to Acme AI with:
- variant header == bucket id, not model version
- cannot force variant by echoing header
- for streaming, read headers on initial response
- best practice: pass stable stickiness key + log headers for debugging

Jamie Alvarez: will do. (reaction) :thumbsup:

Felix Schneider: filed: “Add tooltip + docs link for variant headers in Experiment Results” (will wire to the docs URL once published).

Kenji Nakamura: dropping draft tooltip copy here for quick review:
> “Variant is the experiment bucket assigned by deterministic routing. It is not the model version. For model/version, see the Model breakdown or traces. For streaming requests, variant is returned in the initial HTTP headers.”

Dylan Brooks: looks good. maybe add one sentence: “Clients cannot set variant via request headers.”

Marissa Cole: +1. keep it crisp.

Jamie Alvarez: replying now + will keep this thread updated if they push back.

SupportBot: (reaction) :white_check_mark:

title: #support: 1737839101
preview:
Dev Patel: Heads up - got SIEM samples from VectorGrid (Dedicated) and they’re saying our audit events aren’t normalized across event types. They’re ingesting into Splunk HEC.

Dev Patel: Examples below (redacted). They’re comparing RBAC change vs API key rotation. Fields don’t line up so their detections + dashboards break.

Dev Patel: Sample A (RBAC change) - note `actor_email`, `org_id`, `requestId`:
```json
{
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "time": "2025-01-25T18:10:22.412Z",
  "org_id": "org_7c9f...",
  "project": "proj_91d...",
  "actor_email": "admin@vectorgrid.com",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "requestId": "req_01H...",
  "ip": "203.0.113.17",
  "changes": "role=Admin -> role=ReadOnly"
}
```

Dev Patel: Sample B (API key rotate) - note `tenant_id`, nested-ish actor fields, and `request_id` (underscore), plus no IP:
```json
{
  "event_type": "credential.api_key.rotate",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-25T18:11:03.901Z",
  "tenant_id": "org_7c9f...",
  "actor": {
    "id": "usr_2ab...",
    "email": "admin@vectorgrid.com"
  },
  "request_id": "req_01H...",
  "api_key_id": "key_8f1...",
  "status": "success"
}
```

Dev Patel: Customer expectation: stable top-level fields (timestamp, tenant/org, actor.*, source_ip, request_id, event_id) + consistent naming.

Amina Yusuf: Do we know if these are coming from Hosted vs Private vs Dedicated? (they said Dedicated but want to confirm exporter path)

Dev Patel: Dedicated. They’re using our audit-log-exporter -> Splunk HEC. They pasted from Splunk search results, not from raw exporter logs.

Liam O'Rourke: +1 this came up in the renewal call. They specifically asked for “same fields across all event types” so they can write 1 parser. They asked if we have a canonical schema doc we can share.

Kevin Osei: Yeah that mismatch looks like legacy producers vs v1-ish producers. We should grab raw payloads *before* Splunk does any extraction/field renaming.

Kevin Osei: @Dev Patel can you ask them for 3 things:
1) the raw JSON body our exporter POSTed to HEC (if they can capture)
2) the HEC sourcetype + any transforms they have (sometimes Splunk props.conf renames `timestamp`)
3) at least 5 events across different event_type values (RBAC + API keys + policy change if they have)

Hanae Suzuki: Also confirm whether they’re on exporter version >= 0.14 (the one with schema_version tagging) or older. If older, this may be expected and we can recommend upgrade + normalization.

Dev Patel: Got it. I’ll reply asking for raw exporter payloads + exporter version + Splunk transforms.

Marcus Lin: Please attach the samples + customer impact to a ticket so we can track it. This is going to keep coming up in security reviews.

Kevin Osei: I’ll open a Jira now to track normalization issue and link it back here.

Kevin Osei: Created Jira: SUP-18477 “SIEM export: events not normalized across event types” (assigned to SecEng / exporter). Linked customer thread + pasted redacted samples. @Dev Patel please add the raw events once you get them.

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: will do. Also, do we have approved language for customer right now? They’re asking if schema is “stable” and if they should expect breaking changes.

Hanae Suzuki: For now: “We’re standardizing on a canonical schema and will provide schema_version + backcompat guidance; short-term fix is to normalize legacy fields in exporter.” Don’t promise exact dates.

Marcus Lin: +1. Keep it scoped: we can say we’re addressing inconsistencies and recommend they dedupe on `event_id` once we confirm what they’re receiving.

Amina Yusuf: If they’re seeing duplicates too, we should remind them Splunk HEC is at-least-once and they should dedupe by `event_id` (if present) or (event_type, request_id, timestamp) as a fallback.

Dev Patel: They didn’t mention duplicates yet, just field mismatch. I’ll ask if they’re seeing dupe patterns.

Kevin Osei: When you get raw payloads, please DM me if they include anything sensitive so we can store them in the ticket properly. Redact tokens/keys.

Dev Patel: Will redact. Sending customer follow-up now and will update SUP-18477 with exporter version + raw payloads.

Liam O'Rourke: Thanks. Let me know when we have a customer-facing doc/link we can share so I can close the loop with their security team.

```json
  "requestId": "req_01H...",
  "ip": "203.0.113.17",
  "changes": "role=Admin -> role=ReadOnly"
}
```

Dev Patel: Sample B (API key rotate) - note `tenant_id`, nested-ish actor fields, and `request_id` (underscore), plus no IP:
```json
{
  "event_type": "credential.api_key.rotate",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-25T18:11:03.901Z",
  "tenant_id": "org_7c9f...",
  "actor": {
    "id": "usr_2ab...",
    "email": "admin@vectorgrid.com"
  },
  "request_id": "req_01H...",
  "api_key_id": "key_8f1...",
  "status": "success"
}
```

Dev Patel: Customer expectation: stable top-level fields (timestamp, tenant/org, actor.*, source_ip, request_id, event_id) + consistent naming.

Amina Yusuf: Do we know if these are coming from Hosted vs Private vs Dedicated? (they said Dedicated but want to confirm exporter path)

Dev Patel: Dedicated. They’re using our audit-log-exporter -> Splunk HEC. They pasted from Splunk search results, not from raw exporter logs.

Liam O'Rourke: +1 this came up in the renewal call. They specifically asked for “same fields across all event types” so they can write 1 parser. They asked if we have a canonical schema doc we can share.

Kevin Osei: Yeah that mismatch looks like legacy producers vs v1-ish producers. We should grab raw payloads *before* Splunk does any extraction/field renaming.

Kevin Osei: @Dev Patel can you ask them for 3 things:
1) the raw JSON body our exporter POSTed to HEC (if they can capture)
2) the HEC sourcetype + any transforms they have (sometimes Splunk props.conf renames `timestamp`)
3) at least 5 events across different event_type values (RBAC + API keys + policy change if they have)

Hanae Suzuki: Also confirm whether they’re on exporter version >= 0.14 (the one with schema_version tagging) or older. If older, this may be expected and we can recommend upgrade + normalization.

Dev Patel: Got it. I’ll reply asking for raw exporter payloads + exporter version + Splunk transforms.

Marcus Lin: Please attach the samples + customer impact to a ticket so we can track it. This is going to keep coming up in security reviews.

Kevin Osei: I’ll open a Jira now to track normalization issue and link it back here.

Kevin Osei: Created Jira: SUP-18477 “SIEM export: events not normalized across event types” (assigned to SecEng / exporter). Linked customer thread + pasted redacted samples. @Dev Patel please add the raw events once you get them.

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: will do. Also, do we have approved language for customer right now? They’re asking if schema is “stable” and if they should expect breaking changes.

Hanae Suzuki: For now: “We’re standardizing on a canonical schema and will provide schema_version + backcompat guidance; short-term fix is to normalize legacy fields in exporter.” Don’t promise exact dates.

Marcus Lin: +1. Keep it scoped: we can say we’re addressing inconsistencies and recommend they dedupe on `event_id` once we confirm what they’re receiving.

Amina Yusuf: If they’re seeing duplicates too, we should remind them Splunk HEC is at-least-once and they should dedupe by `event_id` (if present) or (event_type, request_id, timestamp) as a fallback.

Dev Patel: They didn’t mention duplicates yet, just field mismatch. I’ll ask if they’re seeing dupe patterns.

Kevin Osei: When you get raw payloads, please DM me if they include anything sensitive so we can store them in the ticket properly. Redact tokens/keys.

Dev Patel: Will redact. Sending customer follow-up now and will update SUP-18477 with exporter version + raw payloads.

Liam O'Rourke: Thanks. Let me know when we have a customer-facing doc/link we can share so I can close the loop with their security team.

title: #support: 1723849205
preview:
Aisha Rahman: Seeing a customer report come in via Intercom: 'token accounting discrepancy' on chat streaming. Their internal dashboard shows fewer completion tokens than our Usage page. Anyone seen this recently?

Ben Carter: Is this the same pattern where they reconnect streams? If they're retrying, we can legitimately bill tokens twice if we emitted twice.

Irene Choi: +1. Ask if they set idempotency-key. Also check if they have multiple SDK instances behind a load balancer.

Aisha Rahman: They say they don't use idempotency keys. They do have a retry on socket hangup.

incident-bot: Reminder: If customer impact is ongoing, file a SUP ticket and link the thread.

Aisha Rahman: Will file. Also can we confirm whether the invoice export uses the same meter as Usage page? They keep calling it a 'token accounting discrepancy' between those views.

Ben Carter: Invoice export and Usage both use meter_v3; differences usually come from how customers compute tokens from partial chunks.

Irene Choi: Can you share the standard blurb + docs link on streaming token semantics? If not, I can paste from last time.

title: #support: 1712001234
preview:
Devon (Customer): Hey team — since this morning our calls to /v1/generate are returning 401 invalid_api_key. Started after a deploy but we didn’t touch keys. Example response: `{"error":{"type":"invalid_request_error","message":"401 invalid_api_key"}}`. Anyone seen this?
Ava Chen: hi Devon — sorry about that. Can you paste the exact curl (+ headers) you used (mask key except first 4/last 4 chars)? Also the request timestamp and request id in the response if present.
Devon (Customer): sure — curl: `curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_live_1a2b...9z8y" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"input":"test"}' -v` Response had `x-request-id: rq_03f4b2` and body as above. We rotated keys last month but this key was still in our vault.
support-bot: Created ticket SUP-482 for this thread.
Ava Chen: thanks. I tried to reproduce quickly using the partial key you shared — got the same 401 with a matching x-request-id pattern when I hit the same path. Can you confirm whether the app is sending any extra whitespace or prefix text? Common gotchas: copying newline at end, accidentally including `Bearer ` twice, or using `Authorization: sk-...` instead of `Bearer sk-...`.
Devon (Customer): double-checked — key looked right in our pod env var. I pasted the header from a failing request: `Authorization: Bearer  sk_live_1a2b...9z8y` (notice two spaces). Could that break it?
Carlos Ruiz: double space would be parsed literally and fail the auth header regex on our side, yes. But we also see an account-level revoke event at 2024-04-01T15:03:12Z for that key id in audit logs. Ava, can you check audit and key status? @Anita fyi in case this was rotated by security.
Ava Chen: grabbing audit now — confirmed: key `sk_live_1a2b...9z8y` shows status: revoked by admin at 2024-04-01T15:03:12Z, reason: key rotation. That would return 401 invalid_api_key regardless of whitespace.
Anita Kaur: yep that was me — we rotated keys for that org as part of scheduled rotation. I left a note in the org console; maybe the owner didn't see the notification. Sorry about the churn :
Devon (Customer): ah got it. So two separate issues: the key was revoked, and we also had a malformed header in some requests. No wonder 😅. What's the recommended path forward to get us back online quickly?
Ava Chen: quickest recovery: generate a new service key in the Redwood Console -> API Keys, replace the value in your vault, then restart pods (or update env) so processes pick the new value. In parallel, we patched our SDK to trim extra spaces automatically but your client had some direct curl calls.
Ava Chen: repro steps I ran (from support tunnel):
```
# failing (double space + revoked key)
curl -i -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer  sk_live_1a2b...9z8y" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"input":"hello"}'
# response: 401 invalid_api_key, x-request-id rq_03f4b2

# succeeding (after rotate and correct header)
curl -i -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_live_NEWKEY1234...abcd" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"input":"hello"}'
# response: 200 OK
```
Ava Chen: I also attached the audit snippet we pulled (redacted) showing revoke event id `evt_9f2c` and the admin user. If you want, I can keep the ticket open and coordinate a short maintenance window to rotate keys + restart so there’s no drift.
Devon (Customer): please do keep it open. We’ll rotate now and report back. ETA 20m. Thanks for the quick triage.
Carlos Ruiz: FYI for eng — I left a small task to add server-side logging that emits the header parse reason when rejecting auth (without logging keys) so we can distinguish malformed header vs revoked key in future (internal task: INF-215).
Anita Kaur: did we send org-wide rotation notices? I’ll follow up with the comms team.
Devon (Customer): update — rotated key and fixed env var, requests now 200. Thanks Ava and team. Closing the loop. :thumbsup:
Ava Chen: great! I'll mark SUP-482 resolved after a quick postmortem note: cause = admin-initiated key rotation + some clients sending malformed header (double space). Recommendations: regenerate key, update vault, restart processes, and consider short TTL on keys if you want forced rotations.
support-bot: Ticket SUP-482 updated: status -> resolved (1712001899).
